_description: Example seed file for the 'common' project (global rules loaded on every search). Copy this to knowledge/common.json and fill it with YOUR own rules, laws, and patterns. This file IS committed — common.json is NOT.

seeds.content: Law Zero: [your first universal law here — e.g. token protection, syntax conventions, output format rules]
Law One: [your second universal law — e.g. context-aware retrieval, memory lookup workflow]
[Your OS/environment config — e.g. distro, kernel, package manager, system Python path, Node version]
[Your primary language rules — e.g. typing conventions, style preferences, patterns to always/never use]
[Your testing rules — e.g. isolation strategy, mocking conventions, framework preferences]
[Your commit convention — e.g. conventional commits format, length limits, scope rules]
[Your agent workflow — e.g. when to search memory, when to save, how to tag, what categories mean]

Law Seven: prefix development CLI commands with rtk. Intercepts and condenses outputs of git status/diff, find, grep, pytest, npm, cargo by 60-90% to prevent token overflow. Always prioritize local shell commands with RTK over remote GitHub MCP tools for all local repository operations (status, diff, log, file contents). Use github-mcp-server exclusively for remote-only actions (PRs, issues, forks, reviews).

RTK Integration: run rtk init -g --agent <agent_name> to auto-patch agent configs (Claude, Cursor, Cline, Windsurf) for transparent bash execution. Global config lives at ~/.config/rtk/config.toml, generated via rtk config --create. Exclude commands under [hooks] exclude_commands.

RTK Shell Aliases: set aliases in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc for transparent human use (git='rtk git', ls='rtk ls', tree='rtk tree', grep='rtk grep', find='rtk find'). Prefix command with backslash (e.g. \git) to run uncompressed raw binary.
